schools.summary: The address and self-designation: 'unto the angel (the minister, v.20) of the church of Ephesus write; These things saith he that holdeth the seven stars in his right hand, who walketh in the midst of the seven golden candlesticks' — the first of seven letters addressed to the angel of Ephesus, each opening with a title of Christ drawn from the ch. 1 vision suited to the church addressed; here Christ's holding the ministers (the stars) and walking among the churches (the lampstands) assuring his presence and oversight. Barnes and Robertson hold the seven churches literal, historical congregations in Asia, each with its own real condition, the messages (while applicable to all churches) addressed to actual churches.

Victorinus and Bede (and the prophetic-historical tradition): the seven churches, by the number seven (the number of completeness), represent the whole church; some read them as successive ages or types of the church through history. The seven are at once literal Asian congregations and representative of the universal church in all times — what is said to one is said to all ('he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches').

Cyril: Christ, who holds the ministers in his right hand and walks in the midst of the lampstands, is present with and over his churches; the opening title assures the church of Ephesus of his nearness, oversight, and care, the glorified Lord ever among his own.

themes: Unto the angel of the church of Ephesus write; These things saith he that holdeth the seven stars in his right hand
who walketh in the midst of the seven golden candlesticks
the message to Ephesus from him who holds the stars and walks among the lampstands
the literal and representative scope of the seven churches

schools.summary: The searching commendation: 'I know (with perfect knowledge) thy works, and thy labour (kopon — toil), and thy patience (hypomonen — patient endurance), and how thou canst not bear (bastasai — tolerate) them which are evil: and thou hast tried (epeirasas — tested) them which say they are apostles, and are not, and hast found them liars' — Christ knowing fully the Ephesian church's deeds, hard work, and steadfast endurance (commending their diligence and perseverance); their refusal to endure wicked men or false teachers in their midst (their zeal for purity and truth praised); and their testing of false claimants to apostleship, exposing them as liars (vigilant discernment, testing the spirits, cf. 1 John 4:1, commended) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I know thy works, and thy labour, and thy patience, and how thou canst not bear them which are evil
thou hast tried them which say they are apostles, and are not, and hast found them liars
Christ's commendation of Ephesus's works, patience, and discernment
the diligent, enduring, discerning church

schools.summary: The praise of their perseverance: 'and hast borne (ebastasas — endured, carried burdens), and hast patience (patient endurance), and for my name's sake hast laboured (toiled), and hast not fainted (kekopiakes — not grown weary, not given up)' — the Ephesian church having endured hardship, persevered patiently, toiled for Christ's name, and not grown weary (steadfast labour commended); their labour 'for my name's sake' (done for Christ, out of love and zeal for his name, not for self but for him, the right motive ennobling their toil); and not fainting despite trials and toil (their endurance not failing, unwearied faithfulness, which makes the rebuke that follows, v.4, the more striking) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: hast borne, and hast patience, and for my name's sake hast laboured, and hast not fainted
Ephesus's patient endurance for Christ's name
unwearied faithfulness for Christ
the labour done out of love for his name

schools.summary: The turn to reproof: 'nevertheless I have somewhat (this) against thee, because thou hast left (apheskes — abandoned, let go) thy first love (the warm, fervent love of their early Christian days)' — despite all the commendation, Christ having a complaint, the one serious fault marring the church's record; the Ephesians having lost the ardent love they had at first (their zeal for orthodoxy and labour remaining, but the warmth of their first love for Christ, and perhaps for one another, having cooled, correctness without fervent love); the loss of 'first love' no small matter (love the heart of religion, orthodoxy, labour, and patience unable to compensate for cooled love, the decline of love the root of decline, marked by Christ as a grave fault) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: Nevertheless I have somewhat against thee, because thou hast left thy first love
the rebuke of Ephesus for leaving its first love
correctness without fervent love
the decline of love the root of decline

schools.summary: The threefold cure and the warning: 'remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent, and do the first works; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will remove thy candlestick (lampstand) out of his place, except thou repent' — the cure beginning with remembering the former height of love, then repenting of the decline (recollection and repentance the first steps of recovery), then returning to 'the first works' (the deeds that flowed from their first love, repentance issuing in renewed action, the recovery of first love showing in first works); and the threat that the church not recovering its love faces the removal of its lampstand out of its place (the loss of its standing as a church, the withdrawal of Christ's presence and the gospel light, a solemn warning conditioned on repentance) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: Remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent, and do the first works
I will come unto thee quickly, and will remove thy candlestick out of his place, except thou repent
the call to repent and do the first works
the threat of the lampstand's removal

schools.summary: The word of commendation closing the reproof: 'but this thou hast, that thou hatest the deeds of the Nicolaitans (a sect or party teaching error and licentiousness, traditionally traced to Nicolas or named figuratively, whose deeds involved false teaching and immoral practice, cf. vv.14-15 linking them with the error of Balaam), which I also hate' — amid the rebuke, Christ commending the church's hatred of the Nicolaitans' corrupting, licentious error (their rejection of it praised); 'which I also hate' — the church's hatred matching Christ's own (they hate what Christ hates, this agreement with Christ's mind commendable, hatred of evil deeds, not of persons, being right and Christlike) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: this thou hast, that thou hatest the deeds of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate
Ephesus's commendable hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds
abhorrence of the corrupting, licentious error
hating what Christ hates

schools.summary: The universal summons and the promise: 'he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God' — the call to attentive hearing (the refrain in all seven letters, echoing Christ's frequent saying, Matthew 11:15; what is said to one church for all, the Holy Spirit speaking through the message to every church, the plural 'churches' showing the message for all not Ephesus only); and the overcomer (the victor who conquers in the spiritual conflict) promised the tree of life in God's paradise (recalling Eden, pointing to 22:2, eternal life and restored access to God's presence, the first of the seven promises to the overcomers) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: to the one who overcomes, Christ promises to eat of the tree of life in the paradise of God — the restoration of what was lost in Eden, eternal life and unhindered communion with God; the conqueror in the spiritual struggle is granted the immortal life and the presence of God that the tree of life signifies.

themes: He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches
To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God
the call to hear and the promise of the tree of life to the overcomer
eternal life and restored access to God's paradise

schools.summary: The address and self-designation: 'and unto the angel of the church in Smyrna write; These things saith the first and the last, which was dead, and is alive' — to the suffering church at Smyrna, Christ presenting himself by the title from the vision (1:17-18); 'the first and the last' the divine title (Isaiah 44:6) applied to Christ, affirming his eternity (the eternal Lord addressing the church facing death); 'which was dead, and is alive' a title especially fitted to comfort a church facing persecution and martyrdom (the Christ who died and rose assuring the suffering Smyrnaeans that death is not the end, his resurrection the pledge of theirs) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: unto the angel of the church in Smyrna write; These things saith the first and the last, which was dead, and is alive
the message to Smyrna from the first and last who died and lives
the divine title affirming Christ's eternity
the risen Lord comforting the persecuted church

schools.summary: The searching knowledge and the comfort: 'I know thy works, and tribulation (affliction, persecution), and poverty, (but thou art rich) and I know the blasphemy (slander, reviling) of them which say they are Jews, and are not, but are the synagogue of Satan' — Christ knowing their suffering and material poverty yet declaring them rich (though poor in this world, rich in faith, grace, and the treasures of heaven, cf. James 2:5, the paradox of the persecuted poor who are truly rich, the parenthesis correcting the appearance, Christ's estimate not the world's defining their true wealth); and knowing the slander of those who claimed to be Jews (God's people) but opposed and slandered the church — not true Jews but a 'synagogue of Satan,' their persecution of the church marking them as the devil's, not God's (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: I know thy works, and tribulation, and poverty, (but thou art rich)
the blasphemy of them which say they are Jews, and are not, but are the synagogue of Satan
Christ's knowledge of Smyrna's tribulation, poverty, and rich faith
the persecuted poor who are truly rich

schools.summary: The reassurance, foretold trial, and promise: 'fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer: behold, the devil shall cast some of you into prison, that ye may be tried; and ye shall have tribulation ten days: be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life' — Christ bidding the suffering church not to fear the coming sufferings (courage in the face of trial); Satan, working through the persecutors, imprisoning some to test their faith, the 'ten days' a limited, defined period of tribulation (whether literal or a symbol of a short, bounded time, the trial permitted but limited); and the call to faithfulness even unto martyrdom ('faithful unto death'), with the promise of the crown of life (faithfulness through death rewarded with the victor's crown, eternal life, the martyr's reward) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: Fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer... ye shall have tribulation ten days
be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life
the call to be faithful unto death for the crown of life
the trial permitted but limited, the martyr's reward

schools.summary: The refrain and promise: 'he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; He that overcometh shall not be hurt of (harmed by) the second death' — the call to hear (as in all seven letters), and the promise that the overcomer will not be harmed by the second death; 'the second death' being the final, eternal death (the lake of fire, eternal perdition, defined in 20:14; 21:8), distinct from the first, bodily death; the overcomer, though he may suffer the first death (martyrdom, v.10), never suffering the second (the faithful martyr safe, the second death having no power over him, eternal life secure, the comfort suited to a church facing martyrdom) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches
He that overcometh shall not be hurt of the second death
the promise that the overcomer escapes the second death
the eternal death having no power over the faithful

schools.summary: The address and self-designation: 'and to the angel of the church in Pergamos write; These things saith he which hath the sharp sword with two edges' — to the church at Pergamos, Christ presenting himself by the title from the vision (1:16); 'the sharp sword with two edges' the word of Christ (1:16; Hebrews 4:12; Isaiah 49:2) which proceeds from his mouth; the title fitted to a church troubled by false teaching (vv.14-15), Christ coming with the sword of his word to judge and cut off error (warning that he will war against the false teachers among them, v.16, with the sword of his mouth, the title preparing for both commendation and rebuke) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: to the angel of the church in Pergamos write; These things saith he which hath the sharp sword with two edges
the message to Pergamos from him with the sharp two-edged sword
the word of Christ that judges error
the sword held ready against the false teachers

schools.summary: The hostile setting and the commendation: 'I know thy works, and where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat (thronos — throne) is: and thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith, even in those days wherein Antipas was my faithful martyr (witness), who was slain among you, where Satan dwelleth' — Christ knowing their works and the peculiarly hostile place they live (Pergamos a center of pagan idolatry and emperor worship, called 'Satan's seat/throne' for its concentrated evil and persecution); even in this stronghold of Satan the church holding fast to Christ's name and not denying the faith (faithfulness under intense pressure); their faithfulness holding even when Antipas, a faithful witness, was put to death among them (the martyrdom of Antipas proving the reality of their danger and the genuineness of their steadfastness) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: I know... where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat is: and thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith
Antipas was my faithful martyr, who was slain among you, where Satan dwelleth
Christ's commendation of Pergamos for holding fast where Satan dwells
faithfulness in the stronghold of Satan

schools.summary: The fault amid the praise: 'but I have a few things against thee, because thou hast there them that hold the doctrine (teaching) of Balaam, who taught Balac to cast a stumblingblock before the children of Israel, to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication' — despite their steadfastness, the church tolerating within it those who held the corrupting teaching associated with Balaam (the toleration of false teachers the fault); the historical reference (Numbers 25; 31:16) being Balaam's counsel to Balak to seduce Israel into sin (idolatry and immorality) when he could not curse them (the type of the false teacher who corrupts God's people through enticement); the Balaam-like teaching leading to idolatrous feasting and immorality (the same licentious error linked to the Nicolaitans, v.15, that Christ rebukes the church for tolerating) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: thou hast there them that hold the doctrine of Balaam, who taught Balac to cast a stumblingblock before the children of Israel
to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication
the rebuke of Pergamos for tolerating the doctrine of Balaam
the false teacher who corrupts by enticement

schools.summary: The parallel error: 'so hast thou also them that hold the doctrine of the Nicolaitans, which thing I hate' — alongside the Balaam party, the church at Pergamos also harboring those holding the doctrine of the Nicolaitans (the two connected, both teaching idolatry and immorality, Pergamos tolerating what Ephesus hated, 2:6); the 'doctrine of the Nicolaitans' the licentious teaching, like Balaam's, that countenanced idol-feasts and immorality (the parallel with the doctrine of Balaam, v.14, suggesting they are the same or closely related corrupting error); Christ declaring his hatred of it ('which thing I hate,' as in 2:6), the church's toleration of what Christ hates the gravity of their fault (to harbor what Christ hates being to share its guilt unless repented of) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: so hast thou also them that hold the doctrine of the Nicolaitans, which thing I hate
Pergamos's toleration of the Nicolaitans' doctrine, which Christ hates
the licentious corruption linked to Balaam
toleration of what Christ hates

schools.summary: The command and threat: 'repent; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will fight against them with the sword of my mouth' — the church commanded to repent of its toleration of the false teachers; if not, Christ coming quickly and warring against the false teachers ('them') with the sword of his mouth; 'repent' meaning the whole church putting away the toleration of the corrupting error (removing or disciplining the false teachers, not merely personal sorrow); and Christ himself coming to judge the false teachers by the word of his mouth (his word, 1:16; 2:12) if the church will not deal with the error — the sword title (v.12) now turned against them, the word held ready smiting the impenitent (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: Repent; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will fight against them with the sword of my mouth
the call to repent, lest Christ war with the sword of his mouth
repentance meaning the removal of the false teachers
the word turned against the impenitent

schools.summary: The refrain and the twofold promise: 'he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the hidden manna, and will give him a white stone, and in the stone a new name written, which no man knoweth saving he that receiveth it' — the call to hear (as in all the letters); 'the hidden manna' the secret, spiritual nourishment of the overcomer (recalling the manna hidden in the ark, the promise of Christ himself as the true bread, the hidden spiritual food and life given to the conqueror, cf. John 6); 'a white stone (psephos), and in the stone a new name' offering several views (a token of acquittal, as a white stone in a vote; a token of admission or favour; the victor's token) — the white stone with the secret new name signifying the overcomer's acceptance, honour, and a personal, intimate relation to Christ known only to himself, the new name marking the new, glorified standing (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the hidden manna
a white stone, and in the stone a new name written, which no man knoweth saving he that receiveth it
the promise of hidden manna and a white stone with a new name
the secret nourishment, acceptance, and intimate honour of the conqueror

schools.summary: The address and self-designation: 'and unto the angel of the church in Thyatira write; These things saith the Son of God, who hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his feet are like fine brass' — to the church at Thyatira, Christ presenting himself by the title 'the Son of God' (the only letter where this exact title appears, affirming his deity and authority) and by features from the vision (1:14-15): eyes of flaming fire and feet of burnished brass; the 'eyes like a flame of fire' signifying his penetrating, all-searching knowledge (fitting for a church to be searched and judged for its toleration of error, vv.20-23, nothing hidden from his flaming eyes); 'his feet like fine brass' (glowing bronze) the feet of strength and judgment (1:15), fitted to one who comes to tread down evil — the title preparing for the searching judgment of the church and the false prophetess (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: unto the angel of the church in Thyatira write; These things saith the Son of God, who hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his feet are like fine brass
the message to Thyatira from the Son of God with eyes of fire and feet of brass
the deity and all-searching knowledge of the Son of God
the searching, judging Lord

schools.summary: The fivefold commendation: 'I know thy works, and charity (agapen — love), and service (diakonian — ministry), and faith, and thy patience (endurance), and thy works; and the last to be more than the first' — Christ knowing the church's deeds, marked by love, ministry, faith, and patient endurance (a fuller, warmer commendation than Ephesus, who had left their first love, Thyatira abounding in love and service); the church rich in the practical graces of Christian life, especially love and works flowing from it; and 'the last to be more than the first' — unlike Ephesus (whose love had declined), Thyatira's later works exceeding their earlier, growing not declining in love and service, the commendation of increasing fruitfulness (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: I know thy works, and charity, and service, and faith, and thy patience, and thy works
the last to be more than the first
Christ's commendation of Thyatira's growing works of love and faith
increasing fruitfulness, unlike declining Ephesus

schools.summary: The grave fault amid the praise: 'notwithstanding I have a few things against thee, because thou sufferest (tolerate, permit) that woman Jezebel, which calleth herself a prophetess, to teach and to seduce my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols' — despite their growing love, the church tolerating a woman (called 'Jezebel,' after Ahab's idolatrous queen, whether a real woman so named or named figuratively after the OT Jezebel, 1 Kings 16; 21) who claimed to be a prophetess; she, like her namesake, promoting idolatry and immorality, claiming divine inspiration to teach error, and seducing Christ's servants into the same licentious sin as the Nicolaitans and Balaam (idolatry and immorality); the church's fault being its toleration of her teaching and influence (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: thou sufferest that woman Jezebel, which calleth herself a prophetess
to teach and to seduce my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols
the rebuke of Thyatira for tolerating the false prophetess Jezebel
the self-styled prophetess promoting idolatry and immorality

schools.summary: God's patience and her hardening: 'and I gave her space (time, opportunity) to repent of her fornication; and she repented not (would not repent)' — Christ having given the false prophetess time and opportunity to repent of her sin, but she refusing (God's patience preceding his judgment, but she hardening herself against it); the 'space to repent' showing God's mercy even to the corrupt (he does not judge without first giving opportunity for repentance, the delay of judgment an offer of grace); her persisting impenitently in her sin despite the space given, her refusal to repent after being granted opportunity justifying the judgment that follows (vv.22-23, impenitence after mercy sealing the doom) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: I gave her space to repent of her fornication; and she repented not
Jezebel was given space to repent but refused
God's patience preceding judgment
impenitence after mercy sealing the doom

schools.summary: The threatened judgment: 'behold, I will cast her into a bed (of sickness or affliction, a grim, ironic contrast to the bed of her sin), and them that commit adultery with her (her followers in idolatry and immorality) into great tribulation, except they repent of their deeds' — Christ judging the impenitent prophetess with affliction and her followers with great tribulation (the judgment fitting the sin, the 'bed' a bed of sickness/suffering, an ironic judgment on the bed of her adultery, her sin becoming the instrument of her punishment); the judgment conditional even now ('except they repent of their deeds'), her followers (though perhaps not she, who had refused, v.21) yet able to escape by repentance, the threat also a call, mercy still offered to those who turn (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: I will cast her into a bed, and them that commit adultery with her into great tribulation
except they repent of their deeds
the judgment on Jezebel and her followers, except they repent
the threat also a call, mercy still offered

schools.summary: The decisive sentence and its lesson: 'and I will kill her children with death; and all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins (nephrous — kidneys, the inmost feelings) and hearts: and I will give unto every one of you according to your works' — those fully committed to Jezebel's corruption ('her children,' her spiritual offspring, distinct from the followers who might repent, v.22) destroyed with death (pestilence, or sure destruction), the decisive judgment on the confirmed adherents of the error; the judgment making all the churches know that Christ searches the inmost being (the searching eyes of fire, v.18, penetrating the heart, the divine prerogative of searching hearts, Jeremiah 17:10, being Christ's); and the principle of just judgment, each rewarded or punished according to his works (the searcher of hearts rendering to each his due) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: I will kill her children with death; and all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts
I will give unto every one of you according to your works
the judgment on Jezebel's children and Christ as searcher of hearts
just recompense to each according to his works

schools.summary: The turn to the faithful remnant: 'but unto you I say, and unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine, and which have not known the depths of Satan, as they speak; I will put upon you none other burden' — Christ addressing the faithful remnant who rejected the corrupting doctrine (distinguished from Jezebel and her followers); 'the depths of Satan, as they speak' the false teachers' boast of knowing 'the deep things' (claiming hidden, advanced knowledge), which John calls 'the depths of Satan' (the faithful not having gone into these so-called deep mysteries, which were really satanic, their ignorance of the error their commendation); and Christ laying no further burden on the faithful than to hold fast (v.25), not imposing new demands but bidding them keep what they have, his gentleness toward the faithful who are not weighed down but encouraged (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine, and which have not known the depths of Satan
I will put upon you none other burden
the lighter word to the faithful remnant who rejected the error
Christ's gentleness toward the faithful

schools.summary: The simple charge to the faithful: 'but that which ye have already (the faith and good works you possess) hold fast (kratesate — keep, retain firmly) till I come' — the faithful simply to hold fast what they have until Christ comes (no new burden, only steadfast perseverance in the good they already possess); 'hold fast' the call to firm perseverance (to retain their faith, love, and good works without letting go, the faithful needing not to attain something new but not to lose what they have); 'till I come' the holding fast continuing until Christ's coming (whether his coming in judgment on the church or his final advent, the perseverance lasting to the end, the coming of Christ the horizon of their steadfastness) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: that which ye have already hold fast till I come
the call to hold fast what they have till Christ comes
firm perseverance, not losing their faith and works
steadfastness to the end

schools.summary: The conqueror's reward: 'and he that overcometh, and keepeth my works (does the works Christ commands) unto the end, to him will I give power (exousian — authority) over the nations' — to the one who conquers and perseveres in Christ's works to the end, Christ promising authority over the nations (the overcomer sharing Christ's rule); the promise to the one who not only overcomes but 'keeps my works unto the end' (perseverance in obedience to the close, the reward belonging to enduring faithfulness, not a momentary victory); 'power over the nations' the overcomer given to share in Christ's royal authority over the nations (drawn from Psalm 2:8-9), the saints reigning with Christ, the promise of participation in his kingly rule, fulfilled in his kingdom (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: he that overcometh, and keepeth my works unto the end, to him will I give power over the nations
the promise of power over the nations to the overcomer
the reward to enduring faithfulness
a share in Christ's royal rule

schools.summary: The Messianic promise applied to the overcomer: 'and he shall rule (poimanei — shepherd, rule) them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to shivers (shattered): even as I received of my Father' — drawn from Psalm 2:9, the promise that the overcomer, sharing Christ's rule, will govern the nations with firm, irresistible authority (the rod of iron and the shattered pottery signifying the unbreakable, decisive sovereignty, strong unyielding rule that cannot be resisted or broken, the figure of the shepherd's rod turned to iron, ruling and crushing opposition); the overcomer's authority being 'even as I received of my Father' (Christ sharing with the overcomer the very authority he himself received from the Father, the saints' rule a participation in Christ's God-given dominion) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: the overcomer rules the nations with the authority Christ himself received from the Father — a derived, shared sovereignty; the saints reign with Christ by participation in his God-given dominion, the rod of iron marking the firm and irresistible rule granted to the conqueror in Christ's kingdom.

themes: he shall rule them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to shivers
even as I received of my Father
the overcomer ruling the nations with Christ's authority
the shared, God-given dominion

schools.summary: The crowning promise: 'and I will give him the morning star' — to the overcomer Christ promising 'the morning star'; the dominant view taking it as Christ himself, who calls himself 'the bright and morning star' (22:16), so that to be given the morning star is to possess Christ in his glory; other views taking it as the splendour, glory, and immortality given to the overcomer (the bright star of dawn the emblem of the coming glory and the resurrection), the brightest reward surpassing even rule over the nations (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the morning star, herald of the day, signifies the dawning of eternal glory upon the overcomer; Christ gives himself, the true light, the bright and morning star, as the conqueror's everlasting portion and joy — the gift of Christ in his glory the crown of the overcomer's reward.

themes: And I will give him the morning star
the promise of the morning star to the overcomer
Christ himself, the bright and morning star, given to the conqueror
the dawn of eternal glory

schools.summary: The closing refrain: 'he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches' — the call to attentive hearing, the same formula concluding the letters to the last four churches (placed after the promise, whereas in the first three it precedes it); 'he that hath an ear' addressing everyone capable of hearing (all who will heed, the message, though to Thyatira, for all the churches and all believers); the message being 'what the Spirit saith unto the churches' — the Holy Spirit speaking through Christ's words to all the churches, the plural marking the universal application, what was said to Thyatira the Spirit's word to every church in every age (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches
the closing call to hear what the Spirit says to the churches
the Spirit's word to all the churches
the universal application of the message
